title: The Pied Piper Of Hamelin
content:
A CHILDן¿½S STORY.

Written for, and inscribed to, W. M. the Younger.


I.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hamelin townן¿½s in Brunswick,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By famous Hanover city;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The river Weser, deep and wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Washes its wall on the southern side;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pleasanter spot you never spied;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, when begins my ditty,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Almost five hundred years ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see the townsfolk suffer so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From vermin, was a pity.

II.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rats!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They fought the dogs and killed the cats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bit the babies in the cradles,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ate the cheeses out of the vats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And licked the soup from the cooksן¿½ own ladles,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Split open the kegs of salted sprats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Made nests inside menן¿½s Sunday hats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And even spoiled the womenן¿½s chats
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By drowning their speaking
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With shrieking and squeaking
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In fifty different sharps and flats.

III.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At last the people in a body
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the Town Hall came flocking:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis clear,ן¿½ cried they, ן¿½our Mayorן¿½s a noddy;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And as for our Corporation, shocking.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To think we buy gowns lined with ermine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For dolts that canן¿½t or wonן¿½t determine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whatן¿½s best to rid us of our vermin!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You hope, because youן¿½re old and obese,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To find in the furry civic robe ease?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Rouse up, sirs! Give your brains a racking
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To find the remedy weן¿½re lacking,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or, sure as fate, weן¿½ll send you packing!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At this the Mayor and Corporation
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quaked with a mighty consternation.

IV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An hour they sat in council,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At length the Mayor broke silence:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For a guilder Iן¿½d my ermine gown sell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I wish I were a mile hence!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s easy to bid one rack oneן¿½s brain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½m sure my poor head aches again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ve scratched it so, and all in vain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh for a trap, a trap, a trap!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just as he said this, what should hap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the chamber door but a gentle tap?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Bless us,ן¿½ cried the Mayor, ן¿½whatן¿½s that?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (With the Corporation as he sat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Looking little though wondrous fat;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor brighter was his eye, nor moister
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than a too-long-opened oyster,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save when at noon his paunch grew mutinous
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a plate of turtle green and glutinous)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Only a scraping of shoes on the mat?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Anything like the sound of a rat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Makes my heart go pit-a-pat!ן¿½

V.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Come in!ן¿½ the Mayor cried, looking bigger:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in did come the strangest figure!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His queer long coat from heel to head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was half of yellow and half of red,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he himself was tall and thin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With sharp blue eyes, each like a pin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And light loose hair, yet swarthy skin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No tuft on cheek nor beard on chin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But lips where smiles went out and in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was no guessing his kith and kin:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nobody could enough admire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tall man and his quaint attire.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quoth one: ן¿½Itן¿½s as my great-grandsire,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Starting up at the Trump of Doomן¿½s tone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Had walked this way from his painted tombstone!ן¿½

VI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He advanced to the council-table
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, ן¿½Please your honours,ן¿½ said he, ן¿½Iן¿½m able,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By means of a secret charm, to draw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All creatures living beneath the sun,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That creep or swim or fly or run,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½After me so as you never saw!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And I chiefly use my charm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On creatures that do people harm,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The mole and toad and newt and viper;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And people call me the Pied Piper.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And here they noticed round his neck
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A scarf of red and yellow stripe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To match with his coat of the self-same cheque;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And at the scarfן¿½s end hung a pipe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his fingers, they noticed, were ever straying
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if impatient to be playing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon this pipe, as low it dangled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Over his vesture so old-fangled.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet,ן¿½ said he, ן¿½poor piper as I am,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In Tartary I freed the Cham,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Last June, from his huge swarms of gnats;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I eased in Asia the Nizam
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of a monstrous brood of vampire-bats:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And as for what your brain bewilders,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If I can rid your town of rats
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will you give me a thousand guilders?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½One? fifty thousand!ן¿½ was the exclamation
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the astonished Mayor and Corporation.

VII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the street the Piper stept,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Smiling first a little smile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if he knew what magic slept
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In his quiet pipe the while;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then, like a musical adept,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To blow the pipe his lips he wrinkled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And green and blue his sharp eyes twinkled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a candle-flame where salt is sprinkled;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ere three shrill notes the pipe uttered,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You heard as if an army muttered;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the muttering grew to a grumbling;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the grumbling grew to a mighty rumbling;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And out of the houses the rats came tumbling.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Great rats, small rats, lean rats, brawny rats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brown rats, black rats, grey rats, tawny rats,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grave old plodders, gay young friskers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fathers, mothers, uncles, cousins,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cocking tails and pricking whiskers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Families by tens and dozens,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brothers, sisters, husbands, wives,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Followed the Piper for their lives.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From street to street he piped advancing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And step for step they followed dancing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until they came to the river Weser,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherein all plunged and perished!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save one who, stout as Julius Csar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swam across and lived to carry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (As he, the manuscript he cherished)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To Rat-land home his commentary:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which was, ן¿½At the first shrill notes of the pipe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I heard a sound as of scraping tripe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And putting apples, wondrous ripe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Into a cider-pressן¿½s gripe:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a moving away of pickle-tub-boards,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a leaving ajar of conserve-cupboards,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a drawing the corks of train-oil-flasks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a breaking the hoops of butter-casks:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And it seemed as if a voice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½(Sweeter far than by harp or by psaltery
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is breathed) called out, ן¿½Oh rats, rejoice!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The world is grown to one vast drysaltery!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So munch on, crunch on, take your nuncheon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Breakfast, supper, dinner, luncheon!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And just as a bulky sugar-puncheon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½All ready staved, like a great sun shone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Glorious scarce an inch before me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Just as methought it said, Come, bore me!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I found the Weser rolling oן¿½er me.ן¿½

VIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You should have heard the Hamelin people
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ringing the bells till they rocked the steeple.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go,ן¿½ cried the Mayor, ן¿½and get long poles,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Poke out the nests and block up the holes!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Consult with carpenters and builders,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And leave in our town not even a trace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of the rats!ן¿½ when suddenly, up the face
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the Piper perked in the market-place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a, ן¿½First, if you please, my thousand guilders!ן¿½

IX.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A thousand guilders! The Mayor looked blue;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So did the Corporation too.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For council dinners made rare havock
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With Claret, Moselle, Vin-de-Grave, Hock;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And half the money would replenish
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their cellarן¿½s biggest butt with Rhenish.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To pay this sum to a wandering fellow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a gipsy coat of red and yellow!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Beside,ן¿½ quoth the Mayor with a knowing wink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Our business was done at the riverן¿½s brink;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We saw with our eyes the vermin sink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And whatן¿½s dead canן¿½t come to life, I think.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So, friend, weן¿½re not the folks to shrink
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From the duty of giving you something for drink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And a matter of money to put in your poke;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But as for the guilders, what we spoke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of them, as you very well know, was in joke.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Beside, our losses have made us thrifty.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A thousand guilders! Come, take fifty!ן¿½

X.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Piperן¿½s face fell, and he cried
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No trifling! I canן¿½t wait, beside!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ve promised to visit by dinnertime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Bagdat, and accept the prime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of the Head-Cookן¿½s pottage, all heן¿½s rich in,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For having left, in the Caliphן¿½s kitchen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of a nest of scorpions no survivor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With him I proved no bargain-driver,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With you, donן¿½t think Iן¿½ll bate a stiver!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And folks who put me in a passion
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½May find me pipe after another fashion.ן¿½

XI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How?ן¿½ cried the Mayor, ן¿½dן¿½ye think I brook
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Being worse treated than a Cook?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Insulted by a lazy ribald
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With idle pipe and vesture piebald?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You threaten us, fellow? Do your worst,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blow your pipe there till you burst!ן¿½

XII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Once more he stept into the street
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to his lips again
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Laid his long pipe of smooth straight cane;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ere he blew three notes (such sweet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soft notes as yet musicianן¿½s cunning
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Never gave the enraptured air)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a rustling that seemed like a bustling
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of merry crowds justling at pitching and hustling,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Small feet were pattering, wooden shoes clattering,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Little hands clapping and little tongues chattering,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, like fowls in a farm-yard when barley is scattering,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out came the children running.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the little boys and girls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With rosy cheeks and flaxen curls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sparkling eyes and teeth like pearls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tripping and skipping, ran merrily after
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The wonderful music with shouting and laughter.

XIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Mayor was dumb, and the Council stood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if they were changed into blocks of wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unable to move a step, or cry
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the children merrily skipping by,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And could only follow with the eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That joyous crowd at the Piperן¿½s back.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But how the Mayor was on the rack,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the wretched Councilן¿½s bosoms beat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the Piper turned from the High Street
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To where the Weser rolled its waters
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Right in the way of their sons and daughters!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ However he turned from South to West,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to Koppelberg Hill his steps addressed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And after him the children pressed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Great was the joy in every breast.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He never can cross that mighty top!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heן¿½s forced to let the piping drop,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And we shall see our children stop!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When, lo, as they reached the mountain-side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wondrous portal opened wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if a cavern was suddenly hollowed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the Piper advanced and the children followed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when all were in to the very last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The door in the mountain-side shut fast.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did I say, all? No! One was lame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And could not dance the whole of the way;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And in after years, if you would blame
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His sadness, he was used to say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s dull in our town since my playmates left!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I canן¿½t forget that Iן¿½m bereft
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of all the pleasant sights they see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which the Piper also promised me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For he led us, he said, to a joyous land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Joining the town and just at hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Where waters gushed and fruit-trees grew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And flowers put forth a fairer hue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And everything was strange and new;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The sparrows were brighter than peacocks here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And their dogs outran our fallow deer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And honey-bees had lost their stings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And horses were born with eaglesן¿½ wings:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And just as I became assured
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My lame foot would be speedily cured,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The music stopped and I stood still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And found myself outside the hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Left alone against my will,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To go now limping as before,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And never hear of that country more!ן¿½

XIV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alas, alas for Hamelin!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There came into many a burgherן¿½s pate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A text which says that heavenן¿½s gate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Opes to the rich at as easy rate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the needleן¿½s eye takes a camel in!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mayor sent East, West, North and South,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To offer the Piper, by word of mouth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherever it was menן¿½s lot to find him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Silver and gold to his heartן¿½s content,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If heן¿½d only return the way he went,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bring the children behind him.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when they saw ן¿½twas a lost endeavour,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Piper and dancers were gone for ever,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They made a decree that lawyers never
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should think their records dated duly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If, after the day of the month and year,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These words did not as well appear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And so long after what happened here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On the Twenty-second of July,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thirteen hundred and seventy-six:ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the better in memory to fix
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The place of the childrenן¿½s last retreat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They called it, the Pied Piperן¿½s Street
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where any one playing on pipe or tabor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was sure for the future to lose his labour.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor suffered they hostelry or tavern
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To shock with mirth a street so solemn;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But opposite the place of the cavern
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They wrote the story on a column,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on the great church-window painted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The same, to make the world acquainted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How their children were stolen away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there it stands to this very day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I must not omit to say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That in Transylvania thereן¿½s a tribe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of alien people who ascribe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The outlandish ways and dress
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On which their neighbours lay such stress,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To their fathers and mothers having risen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of some subterraneous prison
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into which they were trepanned
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Long time ago in a mighty band
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of Hamelin town in Brunswick land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But how or why, they donן¿½t understand.

XV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, Willy, let me and you be wipers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of scores out with all men, especially pipers:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, whether they pipe us free from rats or from mice,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If weן¿½ve promised them aught, let us keep our promise.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
